description: Protein structure and function rules for therapeutic design - domains, active sites, binding interfaces, and druggability.

facts.context: Enzyme active sites for small molecule drug targeting:
facts.truth: conserved pockets with catalytic residues for binding
distractors: surface-exposed regions lacking sequence conservation
distributed patches across the protein exterior
transmembrane segments shielded from solvent access

facts.context: Allosteric drug binding sites differ from active sites because they:
facts.truth: modulate activity via conformational changes distant from active site
distractors: occupy the same pocket as the substrate in the active site
bind ligands without inducing structural rearrangements
function through covalent modification of catalytic residues

facts.context: Protein-protein interaction (PPI) interfaces are challenging drug targets because:
facts.truth: flat, large surfaces (1500-3000 A²) lack deep pockets
distractors: buried hydrophobic cores prevent ligand accessibility
rapid dissociation kinetics limit compound residence time
high sequence variability prevents conserved targeting

facts.context: PPI hot spots are residues that contribute disproportionately to binding energy:
facts.truth: Trp, Tyr, Arg residues contribute >2 kcal/mol each
distractors: Gly and Ala residues contribute >2 kcal/mol each
all residues contribute equal binding free energy
charged residues at the periphery dominate binding

facts.truth: hinge region connecting N and C lobes, mimicking adenine
distractors: substrate docking groove on the C-lobe surface region
activation loop residues near the phosphorylation site
allosteric pocket between the two kinase lobe domains

facts.context: The DFG motif in kinases determines inhibitor type. Type II inhibitors bind when DFG is:
facts.truth: DFG-out conformation, exposing a hydrophobic pocket
distractors: DFG-in conformation, compacting the activation loop
DFG-intermediate state, partially blocking ATP access
DFG-extended conformation, widening the substrate cleft

facts.context: GPCR orthosteric binding sites for small molecule drugs are located:
facts.truth: within transmembrane helical bundle, extracellular access
distractors: on intracellular loop regions near G-protein coupling
embedded deep within the lipid bilayer interface zone
on the extracellular N-terminal domain surface region

facts.context: Antibody binding specificity is determined by:
facts.truth: CDR loops in variable domains shape antigen recognition
distractors: constant Fc region glycosylation patterns guide binding
framework beta-sheet residues define target selectivity
hinge region flexibility dictates epitope compatibility

facts.context: Protein thermal stability (Tm) affects drug development because:
facts.truth: higher Tm correlates with longer shelf life, less aggregation
distractors: lower Tm improves formulation flexibility and solubility
Tm determines only lyophilization cycle parameters directly
moderate Tm proteins show superior receptor binding affinity

facts.context: Therapeutic protein aggregation is problematic because aggregates:
facts.truth: trigger immunogenicity, reduce efficacy, cause injection reactions
distractors: increase serum half-life through polyvalent receptor binding
improve thermal stability by shielding hydrophobic patches
enhance tissue penetration via increased molecular weight

facts.truth: stabilize structure, require oxidizing conditions to form
distractors: form under reducing cytoplasmic conditions spontaneously
destabilize tertiary structure by constraining loop mobility
require enzymatic catalysis but not specific redox conditions

facts.context: N-linked glycosylation of therapeutic antibodies affects:
distractors: manufacturing yield through improved folding kinetics only
antigen binding affinity through CDR loop stabilization
serum clearance rate through renal filtration size cutoff
